lesson:
The kanji 弟おとうと originally depicted a spool wound with thread, with the thread wrapping downward — the idea of sequence, of what comes after. A younger brother is the one who comes after in the birth order. The same character appears in 兄弟きょうだい (きょうだい, siblings) where 兄あに (older brother) pairs with 弟おとうと (younger brother) to mean siblings in general.

おとうと follows the same in-group/out-group rules as all family terms. For someone else's younger brother: 弟さん (おとうとさん). For your own: just 弟おとうと.

The on'yomi てい／だい shows up in 弟子 (でし, disciple/apprentice — literally "younger-brother child"). The master-disciple relationship in Japanese tradition mirrors the older-younger sibling dynamic: the disciple owes respect, obedience, and loyalty to the master, just as a younger sibling does to an elder.

The kanji 手て in its earliest form was a drawing of five fingers on a palm — a hand, spread open. The modern character simplified but you can still see it if you squint: the horizontal strokes are the fingers, the vertical stroke is the wrist.

手て is a compound machine. It pairs with nearly everything to create new words: 手紙てがみ (てがみ, letter — "hand-paper"), 手伝てつだう (てつだう, to help — "hand-transmit"), 上手じょうず (じょうず, skillful — "upper hand"), 下手へた (へた, unskillful — "lower hand"), 相手あいて (あいて, the other party — "mutual hand"), 選手せんしゅ (せんしゅ, athlete — "selected hand"). In all of these, 手て means less "physical hand" and more "the person who does something" or "the way something is done."

As a standalone body part: 手てを洗あらう (wash your hands), 手をつなぐ (hold hands). But 手て also means "move" in games (いい手, a good move) and "method" in general (手がない, there's no way/method). The hand as a metaphor for capability and action runs through the entire language.

meaning: ten days, tenth of the month
lesson:
とおか. The native number とお (ten) — the same one that caps the ひとつ…とお counting series — plus か for day. After this, the native day-naming system gives up. Days 11 through 19 switch to Sino-Japanese (じゅういちにち、じゅうににち…). The 20th (はつか) stages one last comeback of the old system, then it's regular にち all the way to 31.

十日間とおかかん (とおかかん, a period of ten days) is the duration form — add 間かん to any day counter to turn a date into a span. Without 間かん, it's a date. With it, it's a length of time. This applies to all of them: 三日みっか (the 3rd) vs 三日間 (three days).

口くち is one of the most visually obvious kanji: an open mouth. The character has barely changed from its oracle bone form — it was a rectangle then and it's a rectangle now. As a radical (component in other kanji), it shows up in nearly every character related to speaking, eating, or sounds: 言げん (word), 食しょく (eat), 味あじ (taste), 呼 (call), 吸 (inhale).

Beyond the physical mouth, 口くち means "opening" or "entrance" in a surprisingly wide range of contexts. 入り口 (いりぐち, entrance — "entering mouth"). 出口でぐち (でぐち, exit — "exiting mouth"). 改札口 (かいさつぐち, ticket gate). 窓口 (まどぐち, service window/counter). Any opening that things pass through is a 口くち.

Also a counter: 一口 (ひとくち, one bite/sip). 一口ちょうだい (let me have a bite) is a classic phrase — used between friends, couples, or kids eyeing your food.

The kanji 夏なつ in its earliest form showed a person dancing — a large figure with arms raised and feet moving, wearing some kind of headdress. The connection to summer likely came from harvest festivals and ceremonial dances performed in the hot months. The modern kanji compressed and abstracted the dancer beyond recognition, but the original was pure movement and celebration.

Japanese summer is intense. The country sits in the monsoon belt, so 夏なつ means not just heat but humidity — the sticky, enveloping kind that makes even standing still an act of endurance. 夏休なつやすみ (なつやすみ, summer vacation) is the big annual break for students, and 夏祭なつまつり (なつまつり, summer festival) with its fireworks, food stalls, and yukata is the season's defining cultural image.

Now you have all four: 春はる (はる, spring), 夏なつ (なつ, summer), 秋あき (あき, autumn), 冬ふゆ (ふゆ, winter). The traditional order matters — Japanese lists seasons starting from spring, not winter.

Native counter for seven things. なな (seven) + つ. Straightforward, no sound changes.

Seven has special resonance in Japanese culture, just as it does in many cultures. 七夕たなばた (たなばた, the Star Festival on July 7th) celebrates the one night per year when the stars Vega and Altair (represented as separated lovers) can meet across the Milky Way. 七五三しちごさん (しちごさん, the 7-5-3 festival) celebrates children at ages 7, 5, and 3 — dressed in traditional clothes, visiting shrines. 七転ななころび八起やおき (ななころびやおき, fall seven times, get up eight) is the proverb for resilience.

時とき means "time" or "moment." Double it — 時々ときどき — and you get "at times, at times" → "sometimes." This reduplication pattern is everywhere in Japanese: 人々ひとびと (ひとびと, people — person-person), 色々いろいろ (いろいろ, various — color-color), 日々 (ひび, day after day). Doubling a kanji often means "many of" or "repeatedly."

The 々 mark is called 踊り字 (おどりじ, "dancing character") — it's not actually a kanji but a repetition symbol, like writing "ditto" instead of the same character twice. You can't use it at the start of a line, and it doesn't appear in dictionaries as its own entry.

Frequency hierarchy for Japanese: いつも (always) > よく (often) > 時々 (sometimes) > たまに (occasionally) > めったに〜ない (seldom) > 全然〜ない (never). 時々ときどき sits right in the middle — frequent enough to not be remarkable, infrequent enough to be worth mentioning.

You've already encountered 何なに read as なん — this entry is for the なに reading. Same character, same meaning, two pronunciations used in different contexts.

The rule: use なん before で, の, and counters that start with た/だ/な sounds. Use なに before を, が, も, and at the end of sentences. なにを食たべる？ (what will you eat?) vs. なんで？ (why? / with what?). なにが好すき？ (what do you like?) vs. なん人にん？ (how many people?).

In casual speech, the boundary blurs. Some speakers say なに where the rule says なん, and vice versa. But the general principle holds: なん flows better before dental consonants (t, d, n), なに flows better before vowels and other sounds.

The kanji 人ひと is a stick figure — two strokes leaning against each other, like a person standing with legs apart. In oracle bone script, it was a side view of a person walking: a body bent forward with arms hanging. The modern form is more abstract, but the human silhouette is still there if you see the two strokes as legs supporting a body.

Read as ひと, it's the everyday word for "a person" — general, informal, warm. Read as じん or にん, it forms compounds: 日本人にほんじん (にほんじん, Japanese person), 一人ひとり (ひとり, one person), 大人おとな (おとな, adult). The Sino-Japanese readings じん and にん split roughly like this: じん for types of people (外国人, 日本人), にん for counting people (三人, 何人).

人 vs 方 vs 者: All can mean "person" but at different registers. 人ひと (ひと) is neutral. 方 (かた) is polite — you'd use it when referring respectfully to someone. 者 (もの) is for categories or definitions — 若者 (young person), 学者 (scholar). In conversation, 人ひと covers almost everything.

一人ひとり is read ひとり, not いちにん. And 二人ふたり is read ふたり, not ににん. These are the only two people-counts that use native Japanese readings. From three onward, everything is regular Sino-Japanese: 三人さんにん (さんにん), 四人よにん (よにん), etc.

一人ひとり carries emotional weight beyond its numerical meaning. 一人で (ひとりで, alone/by oneself) is how you say you're doing something solo — eating alone, living alone, traveling alone. 一人暮ひとりぐらし (ひとりぐらし, living alone) is a specific life stage in Japan, especially common for university students and young workers who've moved away from their family home for the first time. The word is so common it's practically a social category.

The most wildly irregular day reading. 一日ついたち is read ついたち — five syllables with no obvious connection to the kanji 一いち (one) or 日にち (day). The reading comes from 月立つきたち (つきたち, "the moon rises/stands") — the first day of the month was when the new moon appeared. つきたち contracted over centuries into ついたち.

Watch out: 一日ついたち can also be read いちにち, meaning "one day" (a duration of 24 hours) or "all day." 一日中いちにちじゅう (いちにちじゅう, all day long). Context distinguishes: if it's a date, it's ついたち. If it's a duration, it's いちにち. 四月一日 (しがつついたち, April 1st) vs. 一日休む (いちにちやすむ, take one day off).

meaning: nine days, ninth of the month
lesson:
ここのか — five syllables for a single date. Native ここの (nine) + か (day). It's the mouthful of the day-counter series, and it sounds so far from 九きゅう read as きゅう or く that learners sometimes don't recognize it as the same number at all.

September 9th — 九月くがつ九日ここのか (くがつここのか) — is 重陽ちょうようの節句せっく (ちょうようのせっく), the Chrysanthemum Festival. Nine was considered the most powerful yang number in Chinese numerology, so double-nine was a day of concentrated vitality. People drank chrysanthemum-infused sake and climbed to high places. The festival has faded in modern Japan compared to its Chinese counterpart, but the date sticks in cultural memory.

方ほう read as ほう is abstract direction — not north-south-east-west, but the "side" you're talking about. Its most important use is in comparisons: こっちの方ほうがいい (this one is better — literally "this side is good"). The 方ほうが pattern is how Japanese makes comparative statements. There's no separate word for "more" — you just point at a direction and say that direction is the one with the quality.

AよりBの方ほうが〜 is the full comparison structure: 肉より魚の方が好き (I like fish more than meat — "compared to meat, the fish direction is liked"). It sounds roundabout in English but it's the natural, everyday way to compare in Japanese. Master this pattern and you can compare anything.

Other meanings: やり方かた (やりかた, way of doing) — here 方ほう is read かた and means "method." 読よみ方かた (よみかた, way of reading), 作り方 (つくりかた, how to make). Verb stem + 方ほう = "the way of doing that verb." Enormously productive pattern.

他ほか is 亻にんべん (person radical) + 也や (a phonetic component). The original meaning: another person, someone else. From there it expanded to "other" in general — another thing, another place, something different from what we're discussing.

他ほかに何なにかありますか？ (Is there anything else?) — you'll hear this at restaurants, shops, meetings. 他ほかの人ひと (other people). その他 (そのた/そのほか, besides that, et cetera) — a phrase you'll see at the bottom of lists and forms.

Often written in hiragana as ほか in casual contexts. The kanji reading can also be た in Sino-Japanese compounds: 他人 (たにん, another person/stranger), 他国 (たこく, another country). The nuance shifts slightly: ほか is "other/else" in a general sense, while た in compounds often implies "not-us" — other as in separate, outside our group.

meaning: I, me (usually used by young males)
lesson:
The kanji 僕ぼく originally meant "servant" — 亻にんべん (person) + 菐ぼく (a phonetic element related to bundling). A servant, someone who works for another. The leap from "servant" to "I" is an act of self-effacement: calling yourself 僕ぼく historically meant presenting yourself as humble, as the listener's servant. That humility has faded in modern usage, but it leaves 僕ぼく feeling softer and less assertive than other male first-person pronouns.

僕ぼく is the pronoun of choice for: boys and young men in casual settings, adult men who want to sound approachable rather than tough, and singers/poets regardless of gender (it's the default first person in song lyrics). Compared to 俺おれ (おれ), which is blunt and masculine, 僕ぼく is gentler — the difference between "I" said with a relaxed smile and "I" said with a chin jut.

Some women, particularly younger ones, use 僕ぼく as a deliberate style choice — it reads as slightly tomboyish or countercultural. But the default expectation is still male usage. In professional settings, most men switch to わたし. 僕ぼく at a job interview would sound too casual, like wearing sneakers to a suit event.

欲ほしい is grammatically an adjective, not a verb — even though English translates it as "to want." This matters because it conjugates like an adjective: 欲ほしくない (don't want), 欲ほしかった (wanted). And because it's an adjective describing a feeling, it can only directly describe the speaker's own desires. You can say 新あたらしい車くるまが欲ほしい (I want a new car). You cannot say 彼は新しい車が欲しい — that's claiming direct knowledge of someone else's inner state.

To talk about what someone else wants, you add がっている: 彼は新しい車を欲しがっている (he seems to want a new car — I'm observing his desire from outside). Or you use 〜たい for verb-wanting: 食べたい (want to eat) works for yourself, 食べたがっている for others. This inside/outside distinction is one of the deepest structural features of Japanese grammar.

The kanji 欲よく is 谷 (valley) + 欠 (to lack, yawn — a person with their mouth open). A valley gaping open — an emptiness that wants to be filled. That's desire in one image.

Dictionaryほしい
Negativeほしくない
Pastほしかった
Past negほしくなかった
Adverbほしく
て-formほしくて

English counts in thousands: thousand, ten thousand, hundred thousand, million. Japanese counts in ten-thousands: 万まん (10,000), then 十万じゅうまん (100,000), 百万ひゃくまん (1,000,000). This means your brain has to shift gears when converting numbers. 50,000 isn't "fifty thousand" — it's 五万ごまん (five ten-thousands). 300,000 is 三十万 (thirty ten-thousands). The unit boundary is in a different place, and until this becomes automatic, you'll always pause to count zeros.

Practically, 万まん is the unit of Japanese daily money. A 一万円札 (いちまんえんさつ, 10,000-yen bill) is the largest common denomination — roughly equivalent to a $100 bill in purchasing power and cultural role. Rent, salaries, and prices are all discussed in units of 万まん. 家賃は八万 (rent is 80,000 yen — eight 万).

The kanji originally depicted a scorpion — the character was borrowed purely for its sound to represent the number 10,000, and the scorpion meaning was abandoned. One of many cases where an ancient pictograph got hijacked for a completely unrelated purpose.

meaning: to be visible, seem, look like, appear
lesson:
見みえる is what happens when seeing happens without effort — things are visible, they appear, they come into view. Nobody is actively looking. The mountain is visible from here: ここから山やまが見みえる. Compare this to 見みる, where you deliberately direct your eyes. 見みる = "I look at." 見みえる = "it can be seen" or "it appears."

The second meaning — "to seem, to look like" — is equally important. 若わかく見みえる (looks young). 元気に見える (seems healthy). 怒おこっているように見みえる (looks like they're angry). When you describe how something appears to an observer, 見みえる is the verb.

Particles: 見みえる takes が — the thing that's visible is the subject, not the object. 富士山が見える (Mt. Fuji is visible). You're not doing something to it — it's presenting itself to your eyes.

Passive/Causative: 見みえる is already intransitive and describes a state of perception. Passive and causative forms exist grammatically but are rarely used in natural speech.

一段 Ichidan自動詞 — pair: 見る (みる)
Dictionaryみえるが
Negativeみえないが
Politeみえますが
Polite negみえませんが
Pastみえたが
Polite pastみえましたが
て-formみえてが
Volitionalみえようが
Polite volみえましょうが
Passive—
Causative—
Caus-pass—
Potential—
Imperative—
Imp なさい—
Cond ～ばみえればが
Cond ～たらみえたらが

The kanji 道みち is 辶しんにょう (the road/movement radical) + 首くび (head/neck). A head moving along a path — someone walking a road, face forward. In the oldest Chinese forms, the head component may have represented a person leading the way, or possibly a severed head carried as a trophy on a military march. The sanitized modern meaning is just "road" or "path."

But 道みち is far more than asphalt. Read as どう in compounds, it means "the way" in a philosophical sense — a discipline, a path of mastery. 柔道 (じゅうどう, judo — "the gentle way"). 剣道 (けんどう, kendo — "the way of the sword"). 茶道 (さどう, the way of tea). 書道 (しょどう, calligraphy — "the way of writing"). In each case, the activity isn't just a skill — it's a 道みち, a lifelong path of practice and self-refinement. This concept comes from Chinese Daoism (道 = Dao/Tao) and permeates Japanese culture.

As a physical noun: 道を聞く (ask for directions). 道みちに迷まよう (get lost — "lose the road").

Quick disambiguation: いつ (when?) and いつ (five) are completely different words that happen to sound identical. Context always handles it — nobody hears いつつください and wonders if you're asking about time. But if you've been puzzling over why the question word for "when" seems to show up in a counting word, now you know: pure coincidence.

The つ-counter series from one to ten is the Swiss army knife of Japanese counting. You don't need to know that pencils use 本ほん, flat things use 枚まい, and small animals use 匹ひき. You just say いつつ and point. It's not elegant, but it works, and native speakers do it too when they're being casual or the specific counter doesn't come to mind fast enough.

The kanji 目め was an eye in oracle bone script — oriented vertically, with the iris visible inside the outline. Over time it rotated 90 degrees (from vertical to horizontal) and the iris lines became the horizontal strokes inside. It's still recognizably an eye, just lying on its side.

As a body part: 目めが大おおきい (has big eyes). 目めが悪わるい (has bad eyesight). 目めが覚さめる (to wake up — "eyes awaken"). Japanese has dozens of 目め idioms: 目に見える (visible), 目を通す (to skim/look through), 目立めだつ (to stand out — "catch the eye").

Suffix use: 〜目 after a number creates ordinal counters — it means "the Nth one." 三みっつ目め (the third one). 一人目 (the first person). 二回目 (the second time). This suffix is one of those small patterns that makes a huge difference once you notice it — it turns any counter into an ordinal.

As a radical in other kanji, 目め signals sight or perception: 見 (see), 眠 (sleep — eyes closed), 眼 (eyeball), 瞬 (blink). Whenever you see 目め inside a character, something related to eyes or looking is usually involved.

The つ counting system is native Japanese — older than the Chinese-derived いち, に, さん. It only goes up to ten (十じゅう = とお), and after that Japanese switches to the Chinese system exclusively. やっつ is the eight in this set, and it's one of the harder ones to remember because the sound doesn't obviously connect to はち (the Chinese-derived "eight" you already know).

The double っ in やっつ comes from a sound change: the original form was closer to やつ, but over centuries the っ crept in for emphasis, the way English speakers might punch the "t" in "eight" harder than they need to. You'll see the same doubling in いっつ → 一ひとつ (though that one's actually ひとつ) and やっつ → 八やっつ.

Mnemonic: ya-ttsu — picture someone yelling "YATTS!" while juggling eight objects and dropping them all. The explosive っ in the middle is the sound of things clattering.

やめる is the verb for quitting — not pausing, not slowing down, but pulling the plug entirely. 仕事しごとをやめる (quit your job). タバコをやめる (quit smoking). やめて！ (stop it!). It's final. Once you やめる something, you're done with it.

Three kanji, one verb: やめる can be written 止やめる, 辞やめる, or 罷やめる — and the choice matters. 止やめる is "stop an action" (stop running, stop talking). 辞やめる is "resign from a position" (quit a job, leave a club). 罷やめる is rare and literary. In practice, hiragana やめる works everywhere and is the safest choice when you're unsure.

やめる vs 止まる vs 止める(とめる): This is where it gets tangled. やめる = quit doing something (you decide). とめる = physically stop something in motion (stop a car, stop bleeding). とまる = something stops on its own. Three different verbs, two of which share the kanji 止. The reading tells you which one you're dealing with.

Particles: やめる takes を — what you quit is the direct object. ゲームをやめる (quit playing the game). When shouting やめて！, the を and its object are dropped because the context is obvious.

Potential がやめられる: が — what you can quit becomes the subject. タバコがやめられない (I can't quit cigarettes) — a very common construction.

Passive にやめられる: に marks who quit on you. 部下にやめられた (my subordinate quit on me). The passive often carries that "and I suffered for it" nuance.

Causative に…をやめさせる: に marks the person you make quit, を marks what they quit. 息子にゲームをやめさせる (make my son quit gaming).

meaning: four days, fourth of the month
lesson:
よっか — say it and notice how the っ makes your tongue bounce, like counting off days on a calendar with a tap-tap rhythm. Four taps. よっか. It shares the よっ opening with よっつ (four things), which is your anchor: if you know one, you know the start of the other.

The first ten days of the month in Japanese are a horror show of irregular readings that just have to be memorized: ついたち, ふつか, みっか, よっか, いつか, むいか, なのか, ようか, ここのか, とおか. After day ten, it mercifully switches to regular counting. よっか sits right in the middle of this gauntlet.

Add 間かん to switch from date to duration: 四日間よっかかん (よっかかん, a span of four days). Without 間かん, 四日よっか means the fourth of the month.

The kanji 夜よる has a person (亻にんべん), a roof-like element, and at the bottom, 夕ゆう — the crescent moon, the evening radical. It's a person under a roof at moonrise. Home at night.

夜 vs 晩: Both mean night, but they carve up the evening differently. 晩ばん (ばん) is the early part — roughly dinner time through the late evening, the social hours. 夜よる is broader: from sunset all the way to sunrise, including the deep, quiet hours. 今晩 (こんばん, tonight — the social evening) vs. 夜中よなか (よなか, middle of the night — the dead hours). When you say 昨日の夜, you might mean 8 PM or 2 AM. When you say 昨日の晩, you almost certainly mean the earlier part.

The word has two readings that matter: よる as a standalone word, and や in compounds like 夜景 (やけい, night view) and 夜行 (やこう, night travel). The や reading is Chinese-derived and sounds more formal, almost poetic. よる is what you use in conversation.

来らい means "come" — and in time words, it means the time that's coming toward you. 来年らいねん (next year), 来月らいげつ (next month), 来週らいしゅう (next week). The pattern is perfectly regular and you already know all the pieces: 来らい + time unit = next [time unit]. That's it.

What makes this worth noting: Japanese conceptualizes future time as something that arrives, not something you go toward. You stand still; next year comes to you. This is the same metaphor baked into 来る (to come) — the future approaches. English does this too sometimes ("the coming year"), but Japanese commits to it across all its time vocabulary.

No particle needed when 来年 functions as a time expression — it works like an adverb: 来年日本に行く (I'm going to Japan next year). You can add に or は for emphasis or contrast, but it's not required.

Six hundred in Japanese isn't ろくひゃく. It's ろっぴゃく. That sound shift — く hardening into っぴ — is the one thing about 六ろく that will trip you up. Everything else is straightforward: ろくじゅう (60), ろくせん (6,000), ろくまん (60,000). But 600 and 6,000,000 (ろっぴゃくまん) insist on the っぴ. Drill those two and you're done.

There's a colloquial expression worth knowing: ろくでもない (good-for-nothing, worthless). It comes from ろくな (decent, proper) + negative, and you'll hear it in frustrated speech: ろくでもない男おとこ (a worthless guy). The ろく here is actually a different word (碌, meaning "smooth" or "proper"), but the sound overlap means you'll remember both.

悪わるい does double duty that "bad" doesn't quite cover. Yes, it means bad in the obvious sense — 天気てんきが悪わるい (bad weather), 頭あたまが悪わるい (not smart — literally "bad head"). But it also means "at fault," and this is where the word gets socially interesting.

悪わるい、悪わるい — said casually, this is an apology. "My bad, my bad." It's admitting fault without the weight of すみません or ごめんなさい. Men use it more than women. It's breezy, almost flippant — appropriate between friends, completely wrong in a formal setting. The doubling softens it further.

The kanji 悪あく has 亜 on top (a phonetic component, not meaningful here) and 心 (heart) on the bottom. A corrupted heart. The same kanji appears in 悪口わるぐち (わるくち, speaking ill of someone) and 悪夢あくむ (あくむ, nightmare). In compounds, 悪あく reads as あく (Chinese-derived) and leans darker: evil, wickedness. As わるい, it stays everyday and manageable.

Dictionaryわるい
Negativeわるくない
Pastわるかった
Past negわるくなかった
Adverbわるく
て-formわるくて

Literally: お (polite prefix) + 手て (hand) + 洗あらい (washing). "The honorable hand-washing place." Japanese has layered its bathroom vocabulary by politeness level, and お手洗てあらい sits near the top — polite enough for any situation, never awkward.

The hierarchy, roughly: トイレ (from English "toilet" — casual, direct, most common in daily speech) → お手洗てあらい (polite euphemism — good for restaurants, offices, talking to strangers) → 化粧室けしょうしつ (けしょうしつ, "powder room" — formal, seen on signs in department stores). Use トイレ with friends, お手洗てあらい everywhere else.

The phrase お手洗いはどこですか is one of the most useful complete sentences in Japanese. It will work in any building, any city, any social register.

主人しゅじん means "master" — the head of a household, the person in charge. Add the honorific ご and it becomes the polite way to refer to someone else's husband. ご主人しゅじんはお元気げんきですか (How is your husband?). You'd never use ご主人しゅじん about your own spouse — for that, you'd say 主人しゅじん (without ご) or, more commonly these days, 夫おっと (おっと).

This word carries the full weight of traditional Japanese family structure. 主人しゅじん literally means "main person" — the one who runs the house. Some younger Japanese women find it uncomfortable and prefer 旦那さん (だんなさん) for someone else's husband, or パートナー for their own. But ご主人しゅじん remains the default in polite adult conversation.

The pair: ご主人しゅじん (someone's husband) ↔ 奥おくさん (おくさん, someone's wife). Your own husband = 主人しゅじん or 夫おっと. Your own wife = 家内 (かない) or 妻つま (つま). Japanese has separate tracks for talking about your family vs. someone else's — always humbling your own and elevating theirs.

Someone tells you they passed a difficult exam. Your response: ほんとに？ (really?). One word, rising intonation, and it works as a complete sentence. That casual contraction — dropping the う from ほんとうに — is so common that the full form almost sounds formal in everyday speech.

The full form 本当ほんとうに carries sincerity that other intensifiers don't. とてもおいしい = "it's very tasty" (just a fact). 本当ほんとうにおいしい = "it's truly tasty" (I mean it, I'm not just being polite). When someone says 本当ほんとうにありがとう, they're emphasizing that the gratitude is genuine. The word's power comes from 本当ほんとう itself — 本ほん (root, origin) + 当とう (hit the mark). Truth that strikes home.

The contraction ladder: ほんとうに (formal/written) → ほんとに (conversational) → ほんとー？ or マジ？ (slang). You can gauge how casual a conversation is by which level the speakers are on.

自じ (self) + 分ぶん (part, portion). Your own portion. The part that belongs to you and no one else. 自分じぶん is how Japanese says "oneself" — and it's remarkably flexible because it absorbs the subject of whatever sentence it's in.

自分じぶんでやる (I'll do it myself). 自分のことは自分でやりなさい (do your own things yourself). 彼は自分が正しいと思っている (he thinks he himself is right). In that last sentence, 自分じぶん refers back to 彼 — it's reflexive, always pointing at whoever the sentence is about.

In some dialects and among some speakers (especially men in the military or in Kansai), 自分じぶん doubles as a first-person pronoun: 自分はそう思います (I think so). This usage sounds blunt and disciplined — a soldier or an athlete might say it. In standard Japanese, though, 自分じぶん is reflexive, not a replacement for 私わたし.

meaning: for, sake, purpose, in order to
lesson:
ため is a grammatical Swiss army knife. It has two core uses that look identical on the surface but mean very different things, and context is the only way to tell them apart.

Purpose: 日本にほんに行いくために日本語にほんごを勉強べんきょうする (I study Japanese in order to go to Japan). Here ため means "for the purpose of." It points forward — here's why I'm doing this.

Cause: 雨あめのために試合しあいが中止ちゅうしになった (the game was canceled because of the rain). Here ため means "because of." It points backward — here's what caused this.

The shortcut: if a verb in dictionary form comes before ために, it's purpose. If a noun + の or a past-tense verb comes before ために, it's cause. 勝かつために練習れんしゅうする (practice to win — purpose). 負けたために泣いた (cried because of losing — cause).

A third, gentler use: 家族のために働く (work for the sake of the family). "For the sake of" — who benefits. This is the most emotionally loaded version. When someone says あなたのために, they mean "everything I did was for you."

You already know 見みつける — to find (something). 見みつかる is its intransitive partner: to be found. The thing surfaces on its own, or gets discovered without you specifying who did the finding. 鍵かぎが見みつかった (the keys were found / the keys turned up). 仕事が見つからない (I can't find a job — literally, a job won't be found).

This intransitive framing is deeply Japanese. Rather than "I found my keys" (鍵を見つけた — which is grammatically fine), natural Japanese gravitates toward 鍵が見つかった — "the keys got found." The focus shifts from the finder to the thing found. It's less about your agency and more about the result.

The negative 見つからない is one of the most useful forms: 答えが見つからない (I can't find the answer). どこにも見つからない (it's nowhere to be found). It expresses frustration elegantly — the thing refuses to surface.

Particles: 見みつかる takes が — the thing that gets found is the subject, not a direct object. 財布が見つかった (the wallet was found).

Passive: Already intransitive, so passive is rare and unnatural — use the plain form instead.

Causative を／に見つからせる: making something get found. Unusual but possible — を leans coercive, に permissive.

五段 Godan自動詞 — pair: 見つける (みつける)
Dictionaryみつかるが
Negativeみつからないが
Politeみつかりますが
Polite negみつかりませんが
Pastみつかったが
Polite pastみつかりましたが
て-formみつかってが
Volitionalみつかろうが
Polite volみつかりましょうが
Passive—
Causative—
Caus-pass—
Potential—
Imperative—
Imp なさい—
Cond ～ばみつかればが
Cond ～たらみつかったらが

meaning: to rest, take a break, take a day off, be absent
lesson:
The kanji 休きゅう is a person (亻にんべん) leaning against a tree (木き). A person resting under a tree. It's one of the most elegant kanji etymologies — simple, visual, and completely intuitive. You'll never forget this one.

But 休やすむ covers more territory than "rest." It also means to be absent — from school, from work, from an event. 学校がっこうを休やすむ (skip/miss school). 今日は仕事を休みます (I'm taking today off work). This is the word you'd use to call in sick. There's no separate verb for "being absent" — resting and not showing up are the same concept.

The noun form 休み (やすみ) means "break, day off, vacation" and is extremely common: 夏休み (summer vacation), 昼休み (lunch break).

Particles: 休やすむ is grammatically intransitive but takes を to mark the thing you're absent from: 学校がっこうを休やすむ, 仕事しごとを休やすむ. This を doesn't mark a direct object — it marks what you're detaching from, similar to how 出る takes を for the place you leave.

Passive に休まれる: に marks who rested/was absent. 部下に休まれて困った (my subordinate was absent and it caused me problems).

Causative を／に休ませる: making or letting someone rest. 子供を休ませる (make the child rest / let the child stay home).

五段 Godan自動詞

Say ゆっくり out loud. The word sounds slow. The soft ゆ opening, the brief pause at っ, the gentle roll of くり. It's one of those Japanese onomatopoeic adverbs where the sound matches the meaning perfectly — a built-in mnemonic.

It works as an adverb (ゆっくり歩あるく walk slowly) and as a standalone encouragement: ゆっくりしてください (take your time / make yourself at home). That second usage is important — it's what a host says to a guest, what a friend says when you're rushing. It's not just about speed. It's about releasing pressure.

ゆっくり話はなしてください is the phrase every language learner needs: "please speak slowly." Keep this in your back pocket.

むっつ is the native Japanese "six" in the つ counting system. Like やっつ (eight), it has a doubled っ that wasn't in the original form — old Japanese said むつ, and the gemination crept in over time.

There's a secondary meaning hiding in this word. むっつり — derived from the same root — means "sullen, tight-lipped, silently brooding." And むっつりスケベ is a colorful compound meaning "secretly perverted" — someone who looks serious on the outside but thinks dirty thoughts. This has nothing to do with counting six things, but once you know it, you'll never mix up むっつ with any other number.

The つ counter covers general objects and only goes to ten. After that, you need specific counters (枚まい for flat things, 本ほん for long things, 匹ひき for small animals). But when you don't know the right counter or can't remember it, the つ system is your safety net — it works for almost anything.

The kanji 花はな is the grass radical (艹くさかんむり) on top — marking it as a plant — with 化か (change, transform) below, serving as the phonetic component. But the phonetic hint is accidentally meaningful: a flower is a plant in its moment of transformation, the brief spectacular change between bud and seed.

はな sounds identical to 鼻 (nose) — same pitch pattern and everything. Context always makes it clear, but it's worth knowing they're different words that happen to share a sound. Some linguists actually think they might share an ancient root: the nose as the protruding part of the face, the flower as the protruding part of the plant. Unproven, but memorable.

花見 (はなみ) — flower viewing — is one of Japan's most important cultural events. In spring, when the cherry blossoms (桜, さくら) bloom, people gather in parks with food and drinks to sit under the trees. The word 花はな in 花見 almost always means cherry blossoms specifically, even though 花はな covers all flowers. Cherry blossoms have claimed the word.

動うごくな！ (don't move!) — if you've watched a single Japanese cop drama, you've heard this line. It's the most dramatic form of 動うごく, and it tells you exactly what the verb means: something in motion, or the potential for motion. 車くるまが動うごかない (the car won't start/move). 時計が動いている (the clock is running). Its transitive partner 動うごかす (うごかす) adds an agent: you make it move.

The kanji 動どう is 重じゅう (heavy) + 力ちから (power) — force applied to mass. That physical sense persists in compounds: 動物どうぶつ (どうぶつ, animal — "moving thing," because what separates animals from plants is movement). 自動 (じどう, automatic — "self-moving"). 感動かんどう (かんどう, being moved emotionally — your feelings in motion). That last one is beautiful: emotion as internal movement.

Particles: 動うごく takes が — the thing moving is the subject. 電車が動いている (the train is running).

Passive に動かれる: indirect passive — someone's movement affects you. 隣の人に動かれて集中できなかった (the person next to me kept moving and I couldn't concentrate).

Causative を／に動かせる: making or letting something move. を leans coercive. 子供を動かせない (I can't get the kid to move).

五段 Godan自動詞 — pair: 動かす (うごかす)

Negativeうごかないが

Polite negうごきませんが

Polite pastうごきましたが
て-formうごいてが
Volitionalうごこうが

Cond ～ばうごけばが

If you ride trains in Japan, you already know this word. 山手線やまのてせん (やまのてせん, the Yamanote Line). 新幹線しんかんせん (しんかんせん, bullet train — literally "new trunk line"). Every rail line in Japan ends in 〜線せん. At a station, announcements constantly say 〜線は followed by delay information. Even if you've never formally studied the word, it's been in your ears.

The kanji 線せん has the thread radical (糸いと) — a strand pulled taut. Threads and lines are the same concept: something thin that extends from one point to another. The compound 視線 (しせん) is my favorite: "gaze-line," your line of sight drawn from your eyes to whatever you're looking at. Japanese sees looking as casting a line outward, almost like fishing.

meaning: seven days, seventh of the month
lesson:
なのか comes from なな (seven) smoothed into なの and then fused with か (day). The irregular day-counting system strikes again — you can't predict なのか from the regular number しち or なな. You just have to know it.

A memory trick: pair the first ten days by sound similarity. 三日みっか (みっか) and 三みっつ (みっつ) share みっ. 七日なのか (なのか) and 七ななつ (ななつ) share なの/なな. The day-counter always echoes the つ-counter, just with か replacing つ and some sound smoothing. Once you see the pattern, the ten day-counters go from impossible to merely annoying.

七月七日 (しちがつなのか, July 7th) is 七夕 (たなばた) — the star festival, when the mythical lovers Orihime and Hikoboshi cross the Milky Way to meet once a year.

When a Japanese menu says お飲のみ物もの以外いがいの持もち込こみはご遠慮えんりょください (please refrain from bringing in anything other than drinks), the 以外いがい is doing all the work: everything outside of what's specified is excluded. It snaps directly onto a noun — no particle between them. 水みず以外いがい (other than water). 彼以外 (other than him). これ以外に何がある？ (what else is there?).

以外いがい belongs to a family of four words sharing the 以い prefix, and they're worth learning as a set: 以外いがい (outside — exception), 以上いじょう (above — "or more"), 以下いか (below — "or less"), 以内いない (inside — "within"). They all work the same way: noun + 以X, no particle. 三十分以内 (within 30 minutes). 百人以上 (100 or more people). Master one and you've got the grammar for all four.

男おとこ is 田た (rice field) + 力ちから (strength). The one who works the field with physical power. It's a kanji from a world where farming was the default occupation and muscle defined the male role. The etymology is honest about its assumptions.

おとこ is the plain, direct word for "man" — not rude, not elevated, just factual. あの男の人 (that man). 男おとこの子こ (boy — literally "male child"). Adding の人 softens it slightly; 男おとこ alone can sound blunt or dramatic depending on context. 男らしい (manly, masculine) uses the suffix らしい to mean "like a man" — a culturally loaded word that's used less casually than it once was.

For formal contexts, 男性 (だんせい) is the polite equivalent — think "male" vs. "man" in English. On forms, surveys, and signs, it's always 男性.

彼かれ technically means "he" — the third-person masculine pronoun. But in practice, Japanese rarely uses third-person pronouns the way English does. You refer to people by name, title, or role: 田中たなかさん, 先生せんせい, あの人. Saying 彼は about someone present would feel odd — almost like pointing at them.

Where 彼かれ really thrives is as "boyfriend." 彼がいる doesn't usually mean "he exists." It means "I have a boyfriend." 彼と別れた (I broke up with my boyfriend). This romantic meaning has become so dominant in casual conversation that the pronoun usage sometimes feels like the secondary definition. The pair: 彼かれ (boyfriend) ↔ 彼女かのじょ (girlfriend).

The kanji 彼かれ has the step radical (彳, suggesting movement or a path) and 皮 (skin, surface) as the phonetic element. "The person over there, walking away" — distant, removed, third-person by design.

In oracle bone script, 女おんな depicted a person kneeling with arms crossed — the posture of deference in ancient Chinese court ritual. Over time, the kneeling figure simplified into the character you see now: three strokes crossing each other. It's one of the most ancient and recognizable kanji, used as a radical in dozens of other characters: 好こう (good — woman + child), 姉あね (older sister), 妻 (wife), 嫌 (dislike), 安 (cheap/peaceful — a woman under a roof).

Like 男おとこ, おんな is the plain, factual word. 女の人 (a woman — softened with の人). 女おんなの子こ (girl). The formal equivalent is 女性 (じょせい), used on forms and in polite speech. おんな by itself, without の人, can sound raw — あの女 (that woman) has a dismissive edge, the way "that woman" can in English.

つま is how you refer to your own wife when speaking to others — it's the humble term, part of Japanese's system of lowering your own family while elevating everyone else's. You say 妻つま about your wife; you say 奥おくさん (おくさん) about someone else's.

The kanji 妻つま is 女 (woman) with a hand reaching down into her hair — the historical explanation is that the hand represents a hairpin being inserted, a reference to a wedding ceremony where the bride's hair was dressed. Marriage literally pinned into the character.

There's a culinary homophone: つま also means the garnish served alongside sashimi — the shredded daikon and perilla leaves. The connection isn't random: つま originally meant "companion, the one beside you," and the garnish sits right beside the fish. The wife is the companion of the husband; the daikon is the companion of the fish. Same word, same spatial metaphor, wildly different contexts.

百ひゃく is visually just 一いち (one) on top of 白しろ (white). One plus white = a hundred? The real story: the bottom part was originally a different character that meant "many" and happened to look like 白しろ. Over centuries, the visual similarity won and people just started writing it as 白しろ. The "one over white" composition is an accident of calligraphic history, not a meaningful combination.

Sound changes: ひゃく plays nicely with most multipliers, but watch for three and six: 三百さんびゃく = さんびゃく (not さんひゃく), 六百ろっぴゃく = ろっぴゃく (not ろくひゃく), 八百はっぴゃく = はっぴゃく (not はちひゃく). The pattern: certain numbers force the ひ to harden into び or ぴ. These are just euphonic changes — the mouth finds them easier to say.

百 also appears in the fun compound 百点 (ひゃくてん, 100 points — a perfect score) and the department store chain name 百貨店 (ひゃっかてん, "hundred-goods store").

へん is vague on purpose. It means "around here," "in this area," "roughly this neighborhood" — without committing to a specific spot. この辺へん (around here). 駅えきの辺へん (near the station). あの辺へんに住すんでいる (I live somewhere around there). It's the word for when you're gesturing vaguely rather than pointing precisely.

この辺 vs この近く: Both mean "near here," but この辺 is fuzzier — a general zone. この近く implies actual proximity. If someone asks where a restaurant is, この辺にあります means "it's somewhere around here, I think." この近くにあります means "it's close by."

The kanji 辺へん has the movement radical (辶しんにょう, the swooping bottom stroke) plus a phonetic element. Movement through a general area — walking around without a fixed destination. That wandering quality is built into the character.

The kanji 店みせ is a roof/shelter (广まだれ) over 占せん (to occupy a space). A space occupied under a roof — a shop. The radical 广まだれ shows up in other buildings: 庭 (garden), 座 (seat), 度 (degree). Anything with 广まだれ tends to involve a structure or a defined space.

みせ is the native Japanese reading, used as a standalone word: あの店 (that shop). The Chinese-derived reading てん appears in compounds: 書店 (しょてん, bookstore), 売店 (ばいてん, kiosk), 店員てんいん (てんいん, store clerk). When you see 〜店 at the end of a business name, read it as てん.

In casual speech, お店 (with the polite お) is more common than bare 店みせ. It just sounds warmer: いいお店知ってる？ (know any good places?).

meaning: to close, be shut, be closed
lesson:
閉しまる is the intransitive "close" — the door closes by itself, the store closes for the night, the window slams shut in the wind. No one is specified as doing the closing. Its transitive partner 閉しめる (しめる) means you actively close something: ドアを閉しめる (close the door).

The kanji 閉へい is the gate radical (門もん) with 才さい inside — a bolt or bar inserted across the gate. A gate being barred shut. You can almost see the physical action in the character.

閉まる vs 閉じる: Both mean "close" but they close different things. 閉しまる is for doors, windows, stores — things with hinges, shutters, or business hours. 閉じる (とじる) is for books, eyes, mouths — things that fold together. 目を閉じる (close your eyes), not 目を閉める.

Particles: 閉しまる takes が — the thing closing is the subject. ドアが閉しまった (the door closed). 店が閉まっている (the store is closed).

Passive: As an intransitive verb, passive is rare. Use the plain form.

Causative を／に閉まらせる: uncommon — usually you'd just use the transitive 閉しめる instead.

五段 Godan自動詞 — pair: 閉める (しめる)
